# Reportability Gates — §207 Cooling-Off & Request for Comment

**Date:** 2026-06-05
**Author:** manual reportability pass (human-authored; not auto-generated)
**Companion to:** `notes/08_external_verification_top_candidates.md`, which closed the
first two gates (prior-role confirmation; client money trail). This note closes the
remaining two: **18 USC §207 cooling-off analysis** and **request for comment**.

---

## Why these gates matter

The scan surfaces a *structure* — a former senior official now lobbying the agency they
served, whose clients receive that agency's money. That structure is newsworthy, but on
its own it is **not evidence of any crime**. Before any of this can be published
responsibly, two questions have to be answered for every case:

1. **Is the lobbying even legal?** Federal law (18 USC §207) imposes timed "cooling-off"
   bans and a permanent particular-matter bar on former officials. If a case clears
   §207, the story is about the *legality and optics of the revolving door* — not a
   violation. If a case does **not** clear §207, that is a far stronger and more
   specific allegation that needs much more confirmation before print.
2. **Has the subject been given a fair chance to respond?** No conflict-of-interest
   story ships without a genuine, documented request for comment to the lobbyist and
   the firm.

This note answers both. The headline conclusion: **on the evidence in our corpus, every
top case clears the timed §207 bans** — the lobbying we observe is legal on its face.
That is the responsible framing, and it is also what makes the *structure* (not a
prosecution) the actual story.

---

## Gate 3 — 18 USC §207 cooling-off analysis

### How §207 works (the four prongs that matter here)

| Prong | Who it covers | Duration | What it bars |
|-------|---------------|----------|--------------|
| **§207(a)(1)** | *every* former employee | **permanent** | Representing anyone back to the U.S. on a **specific party matter** the official **personally and substantially** worked on in government |
| **§207(a)(2)** | every former employee | 2 years | Same, for matters that were *pending under their official responsibility* in their last year |
| **§207(c)** | "senior" employees (senior pay tier / many PAS roles) | **1 year** | Any communication/appearance *to the former agency* on another's behalf, with intent to influence |
| **§207(d)** | "very senior" employees (Exec. Sched. Level I, EOP top tier) | **2 years** | Broader ban reaching the whole former department and other very-senior officials |

The two **timed** bans (§207(c)/(d), 1–2 years) are what people mean by the
"cooling-off period." They expire. The **permanent** §207(a)(1) bar never expires, but
it is **matter-specific**: it only bites if the former official lobbies on the *same
specific-party matter* they personally handled in office. Concentration of lobbying on a
former agency does **not**, by itself, establish a §207(a)(1) violation — that requires
matching a specific current lobbying matter to a specific matter they worked on inside
government. So §207(a)(1) is not a finding we can assert from the corpus; it is the
**live question to put to each firm** (see Gate 4).

### The test we can run from the data

For the timed bans, the dispositive comparison is: **departure date** vs. **first date
the official lobbied the former agency.** Our corpus covers 2022–Q1 2026, so the
"first observed lobbying" date below is the earliest filing *in the corpus window*; where
an official plausibly lobbied earlier, that is noted. Even so, in every case the
departure pre-dates the observed lobbying by **more than two years** — clearing even the
longest timed ban (§207(d)).

| # | Official → Agency | Left agency (approx.) | §207 tier (timed) | Window expired | First lobbying observed (corpus) | Timed ban status |
|---|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Benjamin Steinberg → DOE | ≤ ~2017 (Obama-era EPSA) † | senior — §207(c) 1yr | ≤ ~2018 | 2022-01 (Venn Strategies) | **Clear** |
| 2 | Mark Limbaugh → Interior | 2007 | senior — §207(c) 1yr | 2008 | 2022-01 (Ferguson Group) | **Clear** |
| 4 | Scott Parsons → CFTC | ~2005 † | senior — §207(c) 1yr | ~2006 | 2022-03 (Delta Strategy) | **Clear** |
| 6 | Jim Newsome → CFTC | 2004 (Chairman 2001–04) | agency head — §207(c)/(d) | ≤ 2006 | 2022-04 (Delta Strategy) | **Clear** |
| 7 | Michael Torrey → USDA | pre-2007 (founded firm 2007) † | senior — §207(c) 1yr | pre-2008 | 2022-07 (Torrey Advisory) | **Clear** |
| 8 | Kenneth Barbic → USDA | ~Jan 2021 | senior — §207(c) 1yr (≤ §207(d) 2yr) | ≤ Jan 2023 | 2024-04 (Invariant LLC) | **Clear** (tightest case; 3+ yr gap) |
| 9 | Roger Sherman → FCC | ~2015–16 † | senior — §207(c) 1yr | ~2016–17 | 2022-04 (Waneta Strategies) | **Clear** |
| 10 | Aurene Martin → Interior | ~2005 † | senior — §207(c) 1yr | ~2006 | 2022-04 (Spirit Rock Consulting) | **Clear** |

† **Departure date not yet independently pinned to the month** — confirm against the
official record before print. In each flagged case the *latest plausible* departure still
clears the timed window by a wide margin, so the conclusion is robust to the
uncertainty; the exact date is a fact-checking nicety, not a load-bearing claim.

### Case-by-case reading

- **Barbic is the only case where the timing was even theoretically live.** He left USDA
  around January 2021; the §207(c) one-year window closed ~Jan 2022 (or ~Jan 2023 if he
  were treated as very-senior under §207(d)). The corpus first shows him lobbying under
  Invariant LLC in **April 2024** — over three years post-departure. The one residual
  check: the corpus starts in 2022, so it cannot *see* any 2021 activity. Before
  publishing "fully compliant," confirm Barbic made no lobbying contact with USDA during
  calendar 2021. Nothing in our data suggests he did.

- **Everyone else departed 2004–2017**, so the timed bans expired 5–18 years before the
  lobbying we observe. There is no timed-ban question for these cases. Reported correctly,
  the point is precisely that *the revolving door here is legal* — which is what makes the
  scale of the money (Steinberg's ~$1.08B in discretionary DOE grants to clients;
  Limbaugh's ~$161.7M in Reclamation grants) the story, not a §207 prosecution.

- **§207(a)(1) permanent bar — open for all, asserted for none.** For each official there
  remains a permanent bar on lobbying the *specific matters* they personally and
  substantially handled in office. We are **not** claiming any of them crossed it; doing so
  would require matching a named current lobbying matter to a named matter they worked on
  inside government (e.g., did Limbaugh, who oversaw the Bureau of Reclamation, later lobby
  Reclamation on a *specific project* he personally decided as Assistant Secretary?). That
  matching is beyond what the LDA corpus supports and is the central question for Gate 4.

- **CFTC cases (Newsome, Parsons) are regulatory, not financial.** The CFTC issues no
  awards, so there is no money trail; the conflict is influence over *rules* affecting
  their clients (exchanges, trading and crypto firms). §207 still applies and is clear on
  timing; frame these as rule-making access, not grant-steering.

**Gate 3 conclusion:** All eight examined cases **clear the timed §207 cooling-off bans**.
The only fact-checks outstanding are (a) pinning the four †-flagged departure dates to the
month, (b) confirming Barbic had no 2021 USDA contact, and (c) the §207(a)(1)
particular-matter question, which is a reporting question for the subjects, not a defect
we can resolve from data.

---

## Gate 4 — Request for comment

No conflict-of-interest story ships without a real, documented attempt to let the subject
respond. Below is a reusable template plus the case-specific substance to put to each
firm. **Send to both the named lobbyist and the firm's press/general contact, in writing,
with a firm but reasonable deadline (we use 5 business days), and log the send date and any
response (or non-response) in the story file.**

### Reusable template

> Subject: Request for comment — [FIRM]'s lobbying of [AGENCY] on behalf of [CLIENT(S)]
>
> Dear [Name],
>
> I'm a reporter working on a story about lobbying of the [AGENCY] by former [AGENCY]
> officials. Our review of public Lobbying Disclosure Act filings and USAspending.gov
> records indicates that:
>
> - [Name] served as [role] at [AGENCY] until approximately [date];
> - [Name] is now a registered lobbyist at [FIRM], where filings show [him/her]
>   lobbying [AGENCY] on behalf of [CLIENT(S)] ([N] filings, [period]); and
> - [CLIENT(S)] received approximately [$ amount] in [grants/cooperative agreements]
>   from [AGENCY] during [period], per USAspending.gov.
>
> Before publishing, I want to give you the chance to respond. Specifically:
>
> 1. Is our description of [Name]'s former [AGENCY] role and current lobbying accurate?
> 2. Does [FIRM] have any process to screen [Name]'s [AGENCY] work against the
>    permanent restriction in 18 USC §207(a)(1) on matters [he/she] personally and
>    substantially handled in government? Did [Name] work on any matter in office that
>    overlaps with the current [CLIENT] representation?
> 3. How would you characterize the relationship between [Name]'s prior [AGENCY] service
>    and [FIRM]'s current [AGENCY] practice?
> 4. Anything else you'd like on the record?
>
> I'd be grateful for a reply by [date, 5 business days out]. I'm happy to take a written
> statement or talk by phone. Thank you.

### Per-case specifics

| Firm (contact both lobbyist + firm press) | Lobbyist → Agency | What to put to them | Money figure to confirm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Venn Strategies** | Benjamin Steinberg → DOE | Strongest financial case: discretionary BIL battery-supply-chain grants to a roster of clients. Confirm role seniority caveat (EPSA office CoS, not agency-wide CoS — see notes/08 #1). | ~$1,080,820,047 across 9 clients (16 awards) |
| **The Ferguson Group, LLC** | Mark Limbaugh → Interior | He oversaw the Bureau of Reclamation as Asst. Sec. Water & Science; clients are Reclamation grant recipients. Press the §207(a)(1) question hard here — direct topical overlap between his old portfolio and current clients. | ~$161,687,512 across 19 water districts |
| **Delta Strategy Group** | Jim Newsome **and** Scott Parsons → CFTC | Two former senior CFTC officials (a Chairman + a COO/CoS) at one firm, ~90–96% of filings back on CFTC. Regulatory access story, no money trail. Ask about firm-level screening. | n/a (regulator — rules, not awards) |
| **Torrey Advisory Group** (fka Michael Torrey Associates) | Michael Torrey → USDA | Mostly *routine* program dollars (AMS commodity purchases, FAS food aid) — weaker conflict signal; say so. | Clients incl. Louis Dreyfus $21.8M, Ardent Mills $16.9M (mostly routine) |
| **Invariant LLC** | Kenneth Barbic → USDA | The discretionary standout: Global Clean Energy's $32.26M NRCS Climate-Smart grant. Also ask Barbic directly about any USDA contact in 2021 (the one timing gap). | Global Clean Energy $32,260,500 (discretionary NRCS) |
| **Waneta Strategies, LLC** | Roger Sherman → FCC | FCC Wireless Telecom Bureau Chief now lobbying FCC for wireless/telecom clients (CTIA, Crown Castle, Lynk). Regulatory; note the resolved firm-name reconciliation (notes/08 #9). | n/a (regulator) |
| **Spirit Rock Consulting** | Aurene Martin → Interior | Tribal-affairs lobbying; money trail **inconclusive, not zero** (notes/08). Be careful not to overstate — frame as access/optics, and ask which programs her clients pursue. | Inconclusive (tribal funding flows through varied entities) |

### Contact-finding note

Firm press/contact addresses are on each firm's website (Venn Strategies, The Ferguson
Group, Delta Strategy Group, Torrey Advisory Group, Invariant, Waneta Strategies, Spirit
Rock); the source URLs in `notes/08` (§ "Source URLs") are the starting point for the
individuals' professional pages. Log every outreach attempt — including bounced emails and
unreturned calls — so "[Firm] did not respond to requests for comment by deadline" is
itself documented and defensible.

---

## Bottom line

- **Gate 3 (§207 cooling-off): closed for the timed bans.** All eight examined cases
  clear the 1–2 year cooling-off windows; the lobbying we observe is legal on its face.
  Remaining: pin four departure dates to the month, confirm Barbic had no 2021 USDA
  contact, and treat the permanent §207(a)(1) particular-matter bar as a question for the
  subjects, not a finding.
- **Gate 4 (request for comment): drafted and ready to send** — reusable template plus
  per-case substance and questions for all seven firms. Closing this gate in practice
  requires actually sending the requests and logging responses; that is an editorial
  action outside this repo, but the drafts remove every excuse not to do it.

With Gates 1–2 (notes/08) and Gates 3–4 (this note), the top cases — especially Steinberg
(DOE) and Limbaugh (Interior) — are at the reportable line: confirmed role, confirmed money
trail, confirmed legality, and a fair-comment plan. The story is the **legal** revolving
door and the discretionary dollars flowing through it, framed as conflict-of-interest
*structure and stakes*, not proven wrongdoing.
